---
title: "Delta-method nonnormality diagnostics for coefficient alpha"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: false
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Purpose

This note records a cheap asymptotic diagnostic for the simulation design. The
main alpha-omega note needs nonnormal cells, but verbal claims such as
"residual kurtosis matters more than latent kurtosis" are too vague to use as a
design principle. For coefficient alpha, the first-order delta method gives a
direct population-level decomposition of where nonnormality enters.

The useful object is the asymptotic covariance matrix

$$
\Gamma = \operatorname{Var}\{\sqrt n\,\operatorname{vech}(S-\Sigma)\}.
$$

For alpha, every normality or nonnormality effect is a projection of this
matrix onto one influence direction. This makes it possible to inspect a
population before simulating it.

## Alpha as a covariance functional

Let

$$
\alpha(\Sigma)
= c\left(1-\frac{\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma)}
{\mathbf 1^\top \Sigma \mathbf 1}\right),
\qquad
c=\frac{p}{p-1}.
$$

Write

$$
T=\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma),
\qquad
V=\mathbf 1^\top\Sigma\mathbf 1,
\qquad
r=T/V.
$$

For a small symmetric perturbation \(d\Sigma\),

$$
d\alpha = \operatorname{tr}(B\,d\Sigma),
\qquad
B=\frac{c}{V}\{r\,\mathbf 1\mathbf 1^\top-I\}.
$$

Thus the influence function of sample alpha is

$$
\operatorname{IF}_\alpha(x)
= \operatorname{tr}\{B(xx^\top-\Sigma)\},
$$

and

$$
\sqrt n\{\hat\alpha-\alpha(\Sigma)\}
\Rightarrow
N\{0,\operatorname{Var}[\operatorname{IF}_\alpha(X)]\}.
$$

Equivalently, if \(b=\operatorname{vech}(B)\) is represented in the same
lower-triangular convention as \(\Gamma\), with off-diagonal weights handled
consistently, then

$$
\operatorname{avar}(\hat\alpha) = b^\top\Gamma b.
$$

This is the first diagnostic to compute for each simulation population.

## Independent-source factor model

For the ordinary one-factor congeneric model,

$$
X=\lambda\eta+\epsilon,
\qquad
\Sigma=\lambda\lambda^\top+\operatorname{diag}(\psi),
$$

assume \(\eta\) and the residual components
\(\epsilon_1,\ldots,\epsilon_p\) are mutually independent, have mean zero, and
have variances \(1,\psi_1,\ldots,\psi_p\). Let \(\kappa_\eta\) be the excess
kurtosis of the standardized latent factor, and let \(\kappa_{\epsilon j}\) be
the excess kurtosis of standardized residual \(j\).

Then the fourth-moment covariance of \(S\) decomposes as

$$
\Gamma
= \Gamma_{\mathrm{NT}}(\Sigma)
+ \kappa_\eta\,
  \operatorname{vech}(\lambda\lambda^\top)
  \operatorname{vech}(\lambda\lambda^\top)^\top
+ \sum_{j=1}^p \kappa_{\epsilon j}\,
  \psi_j^2
  \operatorname{vech}(E_{jj})
  \operatorname{vech}(E_{jj})^\top ,
$$

with the same vech weighting convention used by the implementation. Projecting
onto alpha gives

$$
\operatorname{avar}(\hat\alpha)
= 2\operatorname{tr}(B\Sigma B\Sigma)
+ \kappa_\eta(\lambda^\top B\lambda)^2
+ \sum_{j=1}^p \kappa_{\epsilon j}(\psi_j B_{jj})^2.
$$

The three terms have a simple interpretation:

- \(2\operatorname{tr}(B\Sigma B\Sigma)\): normal-theory contribution.
- \(\kappa_\eta(\lambda^\top B\lambda)^2\): latent-kurtosis increment.
- \(\sum_j \kappa_{\epsilon j}(\psi_j B_{jj})^2\): residual-kurtosis increment.

So the latent-versus-error question is not a general verbal rule. It is a
projection question: which fourth-moment directions line up more strongly with
the alpha influence direction \(B\)?

## Tau-equivalent simplification

The tau-equivalent unit-variance case gives a useful check on intuition. Let

$$
\lambda_j^2=q,\qquad
\psi_j=1-q,\qquad
D=1+(p-1)q.
$$

Then

$$
\lambda^\top B\lambda
= \frac{p q(1-q)}{D^2},
\qquad
\psi_j B_{jj}
= -\frac{q(1-q)}{D^2}.
$$

If the latent factor and all residuals have the same excess kurtosis
\(\kappa\), then

$$
\text{latent increment}
= \kappa\,p^2 A,
\qquad
\text{aggregate residual increment}
= \kappa\,p A,
$$

where

$$
A=\frac{q^2(1-q)^2}{D^4}.
$$

In this clean tau-equivalent model, latent kurtosis contributes \(p\) times as
much as the aggregate residual-kurtosis term when excess kurtoses are equal.
This cuts against a blanket claim that residual kurtosis is generally more
important for alpha. Residual kurtosis can dominate only if its excess kurtosis
or its projection terms are sufficiently larger.

## Dependent but uncorrelated sources

The decomposition above uses mutual independence. That assumption is doing real
work. If the latent factor and residuals, or different residuals, are dependent
but uncorrelated, the covariance model

$$
\Sigma=\lambda\lambda^\top+\operatorname{diag}(\psi)
$$

can still hold, but \(\Gamma\) gains mixed fourth-cumulant terms. The alpha
variance remains

$$
\operatorname{avar}(\hat\alpha)
= \operatorname{Var}\{\operatorname{tr}[B(XX^\top-\Sigma)]\},
$$

but it no longer separates cleanly into one latent-kurtosis term plus \(p\)
residual-kurtosis terms. Cross terms such as
\(\operatorname{cum}(\eta,\eta,\epsilon_j,\epsilon_k)\) and
\(\operatorname{cum}(\epsilon_i,\epsilon_j,\epsilon_k,\epsilon_l)\) may enter.

This is a possible robustness stress design, but it is not the clean default
for the alpha-omega paper. Most reliability simulations that discuss "latent"
or "error" nonnormality are easier to interpret as independent-source designs:
make the common factor nonnormal, make residuals nonnormal, or make both
nonnormal while preserving the same covariance model.

Correlated residuals are a different and more standard SEM misspecification:
they change \(\Sigma\) itself by adding off-diagonal residual covariance. Those
belong in the non-congeneric/misspecified-model simulation block, not in the
independent-source kurtosis decomposition.

## Simulation-design implication

For the main paper, the recommended split is:

1. Keep the main congeneric cells as independent-source factor models. They are
   mathematically transparent, and the normal versus nonnormal contrast is
   explainable through \(\Gamma\).
2. Add a small asymptotic diagnostic table for selected populations:
   normal-theory alpha variance, latent-kurtosis increment, residual-kurtosis
   increment, and their proportions.
3. Treat correlated residuals, minor factors, and two-factor populations as
   model-misspecification cells. They answer a different question: whether
   reliability-gap tests and structural tau-equivalence tests diverge when the
   fitted one-factor model is false.
4. Leave dependent-but-uncorrelated sources out of the first paper unless a
   specific literature design requires them. They are mathematically possible,
   but they obscure the main point and require mixed fourth-cumulant
   bookkeeping rather than the simple low-rank \(\Gamma\) decomposition above.
